past_medical_history: Chronic low back pain with two lumbar disc herniations (L4-L5, L5-S1) on MRI three years ago, managed conservatively
Irritable bowel syndrome diagnosed at age 27
Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 25
Major depressive episode at age 34, treated with sertraline, in partial remission
Childhood sexual abuse with PTSD, treated with psychotherapy intermittently

medications.indication: Muscle spasm related to chronic back pain

medications.frequency: As needed, up to three times daily

family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Maternal aunt with multiple sclerosis diagnosed in her 30s
Paternal grandmother with Parkinson's disease, onset in her 70s
No known family history of epilepsy
One sister (age 37) with chronic fatigue syndrome

social_history.occupation: Former administrative assistant, on long-term disability for 14 months due to progressive leg weakness
social_history.alcohol: Previously social drinker, stopped 6 months ago due to medication interactions
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 5 years ago, 8 pack-year history
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies any current or recent illicit drug use; remote marijuana use in college
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two children (ages 8 and 11) in a single-story home; husband has taken on most household responsibilities; patient uses wheelchair for mobility
social_history.exercise: Unable to exercise due to leg weakness; previously enjoyed hiking and swimming
social_history.psychosocial_stressors: Ongoing marital strain due to disability and financial difficulties; eldest child recently diagnosed with ADHD requiring additional support; mother-in-law moved in 18 months ago creating household tension

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect mildly flat, somewhat guarded in responses. Speech fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Attention adequate on digit span (6 forward, 4 backward). Memory 3/3 registration, 2/3 recall at 5 minutes (recalled third with category cue). Clock drawing normal. Montreal Cognitive Assessment 26/30 (lost points on delayed recall and serial 7s).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. Mild subjective decreased sensation over left V2 distribution, but sensation objectively intact on formal testing. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: Normal bulk and tone. Strength 5/5 throughout bilaterally. No pronator drift. Lower extremities: Mildly decreased bulk bilaterally attributed to disuse, but no fasciculations. Tone normal without spasticity or rigidity. On direct strength testing, patient demonstrates 2/5 hip flexion, 2/5 knee extension, 3/5 ankle dorsiflexion, and 3/5 ankle plantarflexion bilaterally. However, strength testing is notable for inconsistency: give-way pattern observed with sudden collapse of resistance during hip flexion and knee extension bilaterally. Patient able to lift legs briefly off bed against gravity but then they drop suddenly rather than gradually weakening. Notably, when attention is diverted during seated position, patient observed to shift both legs and use hip flexors with apparently greater force than demonstrated during formal testing.
neurological_exam.sensory: Upper extremities: Intact to all modalities. Lower extremities: Patient reports decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a non-dermatomal, non-length-dependent distribution with a sharp demarcation at the bilateral inguinal creases. Proprioception testing at great toes shows exaggerated sway-like responses with large, irregular errors rather than consistent deficit. Vibration sense reportedly absent at bilateral medial malleoli but intact at bilateral tibial tubercles. Temperature sensation intact throughout.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps, triceps, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Upper extremities: Finger-to-nose intact without dysmetria. Lower extremities: Patient reports inability to perform heel-to-shin due to weakness, but during the attempt, the heel is placed accurately on the contralateral knee, suggesting intact coordination when movement is possible.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient is wheelchair-bound and states she has been unable to walk for 6 weeks. When assisted to standing by two people, she demonstrates dramatic buckling of both knees without actually falling, catching herself by gripping the wheelchair arms. She states her legs cannot support her weight. Tandem and Romberg not testable per patient report.
neurological_exam.additional: Hoover sign testing performed: When asked to flex the right hip against resistance, no downward pressure is felt under the contralateral heel. However, when asked to flex the LEFT hip against resistance, strong involuntary downward pressure is felt under the right heel, demonstrating involuntary extension of the right leg that is not produced during voluntary testing. The same pattern is reproducible on the opposite side. This constitutes a positive Hoover sign bilaterally, though the finding is subtle and could be missed without specific attention. No tremor, no myoclonus. No dystonic posturing. Abdominal cutaneous reflexes present and symmetric.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive bilateral leg weakness over 8 weeks, now unable to walk and wheelchair-dependent
patient.history_present_illness:
Mrs. Kowalski is a 40-year-old right-handed woman with a significant past medical history of fibromyalgia, chronic low back pain with documented lumbar disc herniations, and depression who is admitted for evaluation of progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness that has rendered her wheelchair-bound over the past 8 weeks. The weakness began insidiously approximately 3 months ago as a sensation of heaviness in both legs, initially more prominent on the right side, which she attributed to worsening of her chronic back pain. Over the following weeks, she noticed progressive difficulty climbing stairs, rising from seated positions, and walking more than short distances. Her outpatient primary care physician ordered a lumbar spine MRI which showed stable disc herniations without significant canal stenosis or new nerve root compression, unchanged from imaging 3 years prior.

Despite physical therapy and escalation of her gabapentin dose, the weakness continued to progress. Approximately 6 weeks ago, she experienced what she describes as a sudden worsening after a particularly stressful weekend involving a heated argument with her husband about finances and her mother-in-law's living arrangements. She woke the following morning unable to stand without assistance and has been wheelchair-dependent since. She denies any bowel or bladder incontinence but reports urinary urgency that she attributes to anxiety. She denies back pain radiation, saddle anesthesia, or any new sensory symptoms other than a vague numbness in both legs from the groin down that she says comes and goes.

She was initially evaluated in the emergency department 5 weeks ago where a CT head was unremarkable and she was discharged with outpatient neurology follow-up. The outpatient neurologist noted findings concerning for possible spinal cord pathology versus neuromuscular disease and recommended admission for expedited workup including MRI of the brain and spine, lumbar puncture, EMG/NCS, and comprehensive laboratory evaluation. The family is very concerned and her husband has been researching multiple sclerosis online, noting the family history of MS in her maternal aunt. The patient herself is tearful and states she is terrified she has a progressive neurological disease. She reports worsening depression and increased anxiety since the onset of weakness. She has had no fevers, weight loss, or systemic symptoms. She denies any prior episodes of neurological symptoms other than chronic pain.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz well-organized alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Drowsiness with vertex waves and sleep spindles captured; no abnormalities in sleep

artifacts.effect: Minor, does not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No change in background activity, no epileptiform discharges provoked
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving response, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities identified. No focal slowing. This is a normal study and does not exclude a clinical diagnosis of seizures, but there is no electrographic evidence to support epilepsy.
eeg.limitations: No clinical events occurred during the recording period.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense on T2, suppresses on FLAIR

additional_observations: Ventricular system normal in size and configuration
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
mri.impression: Essentially normal brain MRI. Incidental small left middle cranial fossa arachnoid cyst, clinically insignificant. No evidence of demyelination, infarction, mass lesion, or other structural abnormality to explain the patient's symptoms.
recommended_actions: No follow-up imaging needed for arachnoid cyst unless new symptoms develop

Vitamins.clinical_significance: Mild vitamin D insufficiency, common in the general population and unlikely to account for the degree of weakness described

Autoimmune_Screen.clinical_significance: Low-titer ANA (1:80) is found in up to 15% of healthy individuals and is of uncertain clinical significance in the absence of other features of connective tissue disease

labs.interpretation: Comprehensive laboratory evaluation is essentially unremarkable. Low-titer ANA (1:80 speckled) is a nonspecific finding present in a significant proportion of healthy individuals. Mild vitamin D insufficiency is noted but does not explain the clinical picture. CK and aldolase are normal, arguing against myopathy. AChR antibodies are negative, making myasthenia gravis very unlikely. NMO-IgG is negative. Inflammatory markers are within normal limits.
abnormal_values_summary: Vitamin D 25-OH: 22 ng/mL (low, reference 30-100) - mild insufficiency
ANA: 1:80 speckled (positive at low titer) - nonspecific finding

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No ST-T wave changes. No conduction abnormalities. Normal ECG.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized and reactive
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, sleep spindles, and slow wave sleep captured

findings.clinical_correlation: Day 2 of monitoring: Patient reported sudden onset of leg heaviness and was observed to have bilateral leg shaking lasting approximately 90 seconds. During the event, EEG showed normal awake background with movement artifact. No epileptiform activity, no electrographic seizure pattern, no post-event slowing. Heart rate remained stable at 82 bpm.

Day 3 of monitoring: Patient had episode of bilateral leg weakness with inability to move legs for approximately 4 minutes, followed by crying. EEG throughout the event showed persistent normal alpha rhythm with no change from baseline. No epileptiform activity. No postictal changes observed.

artifacts.period: During clinical event 1, corresponding to observed leg shaking

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed on Day 1 - no abnormalities provoked
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric driving response, no photoparoxysmal response
activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: Performed night of Day 2 - no epileptiform discharges in post-deprivation recording

output.impression: Normal 72-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Two habitual clinical events were captured, both without any EEG correlate. The first event consisted of bilateral leg shaking and the second consisted of bilateral leg weakness with emotional distress. The absence of EEG changes during these clinical events is inconsistent with epileptic seizures and is consistent with psychogenic non-epileptic events (functional neurological symptom events). No epileptiform abnormalities were identified during the entire monitoring period including sleep and sleep deprivation.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with functional neurological disorder
No indication for antiepileptic drug therapy

EMG_Needle_Examination.value: Reduced recruitment with rapid firing rate
EMG_Needle_Examination.clinical_significance: Reduced recruitment without denervation potentials, with motor unit potentials of normal morphology and rapid firing, suggests poor voluntary activation rather than a neurogenic or myopathic process

EMG_Needle_Examination.value: Reduced recruitment with rapid firing rate
EMG_Needle_Examination.clinical_significance: Same pattern as tibialis anterior - poor voluntary activation without evidence of denervation

EMG_Needle_Examination.value: Mildly reduced with normal MUP morphology
EMG_Needle_Examination.clinical_significance: Consistent with submaximal effort rather than neurogenic process

output.interpretation: EMG and nerve conduction studies of the bilateral lower extremities are NORMAL. Motor and sensory nerve conduction studies show normal amplitudes, latencies, and conduction velocities with no evidence of demyelination or axonal loss. Needle EMG demonstrates no evidence of denervation (no fibrillations, positive sharp waves, or fasciculations) in any muscle tested. Motor unit potential morphology is normal throughout. The finding of reduced recruitment with rapid firing rates and normal MUP morphology in several lower extremity muscles is consistent with poor voluntary activation or submaximal effort, and does NOT suggest a neurogenic or myopathic process. F-wave latencies are within normal limits. These findings effectively exclude peripheral neuropathy, radiculopathy with active denervation, myopathy, and motor neuron disease as causes of the patient's weakness.
abnormal_values_summary: Reduced recruitment in right tibialis anterior, vastus medialis, and medial gastrocnemius - pattern consistent with submaximal effort/poor voluntary activation

signal_characteristics.T2: Normal cord signal throughout, no myelopathy signal
Decreased signal in L4-L5 and L5-S1 discs consistent with desiccation

findings.mass_effect: Minimal ventral thecal sac effacement, no nerve root compression
Contacts ventral thecal sac without significant nerve root compression. No cauda equina compression.

additional_observations: Conus medullaris terminates at L1-L2 level, normal position
No abnormal signal within the conus
output.impression: Normal cervical and thoracic spinal cord without evidence of demyelination, myelopathy, or compressive lesion. Lumbar spine demonstrates stable mild degenerative changes at L4-L5 and L5-S1 as previously documented, without significant neural foraminal narrowing or nerve root compression. The lumbar findings do not adequately explain the degree of bilateral lower extremity weakness described clinically. No evidence of cauda equina syndrome.
recommended_actions: Lumbar findings are stable and not concordant with the severity of clinical presentation
No neurosurgical intervention indicated based on imaging

Cognitive_Domains.clinical_significance: Mildly low processing speed, consistent with effects of pain, medications (gabapentin, duloxetine), depression, and/or poor effort rather than a neurodegenerative process

Symptom_Validity.clinical_significance: Borderline performance on symptom validity testing suggests possible suboptimal effort or symptom exaggeration, though does not reach definitive threshold for invalid performance

Psychological_Measures.clinical_significance: Score above clinical cutoff for probable PTSD

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological evaluation reveals overall average cognitive functioning with no evidence of a neurodegenerative process. Mild reduction in processing speed is consistent with the combined effects of chronic pain, sedating medications, and depression rather than organic neurological disease. Memory functioning is intact across verbal and visual domains. Symptom validity testing showed borderline Trial 1 performance that normalized on Trial 2, suggesting possible initial poor effort that improved with encouragement. Psychological screening reveals moderate depression, moderate anxiety, and probable PTSD. These psychiatric comorbidities are clinically significant and may be contributing to or maintaining functional neurological symptoms.
abnormal_values_summary: Processing Speed Index: 88 (Low Average) - medication/mood effects likely
TOMM Trial 1: 38/50 (borderline effort on initial trial)

Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Moderately severe depressive symptoms with prominent somatic features including fatigue, sleep disturbance, and psychomotor retardation
Moderate generalized anxiety with significant health-related worry
Elevated dissociative experiences, consistent with trauma history and functional neurological disorder
Significant adverse childhood experiences including sexual abuse, emotional neglect, and parental substance abuse; strong risk factor for functional neurological disorder

Clinical_Interview_Summary.value: Confirmed childhood sexual abuse ages 8-12 by family friend; previously disclosed in therapy but has not completed trauma-focused treatment; reports intrusive memories have worsened since onset of physical symptoms
Marital conflict, financial strain from disability, role loss, mother-in-law conflict, child's behavioral difficulties
Clinical_Interview_Summary.clinical_significance: Multiple concurrent stressors likely perpetuating symptoms

Clinical_Interview_Summary.value: Patient firmly believes she has an undiagnosed neurological disease; expresses frustration with physicians who she feels are not taking her symptoms seriously; resistant to psychological explanations
Clinical_Interview_Summary.clinical_significance: Strong biomedical illness attribution may be a barrier to treatment engagement

output.interpretation: Comprehensive psychiatric evaluation reveals moderately severe depression with prominent somatic features, moderate generalized anxiety, elevated dissociative experiences, and a significant history of adverse childhood experiences including sexual abuse. The patient meets criteria for major depressive disorder (moderate, recurrent), generalized anxiety disorder, and likely PTSD with prominent dissociative features. The temporal relationship between escalating psychosocial stressors and the progressive worsening of neurological symptoms is clinically significant. The patient's strong biomedical illness attribution and resistance to psychological formulation are notable and will require sensitive, collaborative therapeutic engagement. The psychiatric profile is highly consistent with predisposing and perpetuating factors for functional neurological disorder.
abnormal_values_summary: Unresolved PTSD related to childhood sexual abuse

output.interpretation: Cerebrospinal fluid analysis is entirely normal. Opening pressure is normal. Cell count, protein, and glucose are all within normal limits. Oligoclonal bands are absent and IgG index is normal, effectively excluding multiple sclerosis and other inflammatory CNS conditions. Cytology is negative for malignancy. MOG antibody is negative. These findings do not support an inflammatory, infectious, or neoplastic process affecting the central nervous system.

Functional_Signs_Assessment.clinical_significance: Involuntary hip extension present during contralateral hip flexion, not produced during voluntary testing. Positive Hoover sign is a validated, specific clinical sign for functional leg weakness.
Same pattern on contralateral side, confirming bilateral positive Hoover sign
When testing hip abduction against resistance on one side, involuntary abduction is felt on the contralateral side, contradicting the reported bilateral weakness

Functional_Signs_Assessment.value: Present bilaterally in hip flexors, knee extensors
Functional_Signs_Assessment.clinical_significance: Sudden collapse of resistance during strength testing rather than gradual fatigue, inconsistent with organic weakness

Functional_Signs_Assessment.test: Inconsistency in Observed vs Tested Strength
Functional_Signs_Assessment.clinical_significance: Patient demonstrates better motor function during activities not framed as strength tests (e.g., shifting in wheelchair, repositioning legs) compared to formal testing

Functional_Signs_Assessment.value: Present - sharp demarcation at bilateral inguinal creases
Functional_Signs_Assessment.reference_range: Dermatomal or length-dependent pattern expected in organic disease
Functional_Signs_Assessment.clinical_significance: Sensory loss with sharp cutoff at inguinal crease does not conform to any dermatomal, peripheral nerve, or spinal cord level distribution

output.interpretation: Comprehensive functional neurological assessment reveals multiple positive clinical signs diagnostic of functional neurological disorder. Bilateral positive Hoover sign, positive hip abductor sign, give-way weakness pattern, inconsistency between observed and formally tested strength, and non-anatomical sensory examination all represent rule-in diagnostic signs for FND. These are not signs of exclusion but rather positive diagnostic markers with high specificity for functional weakness. Taken together with the entirely normal EMG/NCS, normal spine MRI, and normal laboratory workup, the clinical picture is consistent with functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness.
abnormal_values_summary: Inconsistency in observed vs tested strength

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness

differential.key_features: Family history of MS in maternal aunt raised clinical suspicion. However, normal brain and spinal cord MRI, absent oligoclonal bands, normal IgG index, and lack of clinical dissemination in time and space effectively exclude this diagnosis.

Known lumbar disc herniations could theoretically cause leg weakness, but imaging shows stable minimal changes without significant nerve root compression, EMG shows no denervation, and the bilateral symmetric pattern does not fit a radicular distribution.
Bilateral proximal weakness could suggest myopathy. However, CK and aldolase are normal, EMG shows no myopathic changes, and the give-way pattern is inconsistent with true myopathic weakness.
Negative AChR antibodies. No fatigable weakness, ptosis, or bulbar symptoms. Fixed rather than fatigable weakness pattern.
Age and progressive weakness could raise concern, but EMG shows no denervation or fasciculations, reflexes are normal (not brisk), and no upper motor neuron signs are present.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including timeline of weakness progression, psychosocial stressors, and psychiatric history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive bilateral leg weakness temporally associated with psychosocial stressors; history of fibromyalgia, chronic pain, depression, and childhood trauma

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with specific attention to functional signs (Hoover sign, hip abductor sign, give-way weakness, inconsistency in tested vs observed function)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign bilaterally, give-way weakness, inconsistent motor performance, non-anatomical sensory loss

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with gadolinium to exclude structural or demyelinating disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI with incidental arachnoid cyst

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine to exclude myelopathy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal cervical and thoracic cord; stable mild lumbar degenerative changes not explaining clinical picture

optimal_actions.action: Order EMG/nerve conduction studies to exclude peripheral neuropathy, radiculopathy, and myopathy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal NCS, EMG showing reduced recruitment consistent with poor voluntary activation rather than neurogenic or myopathic process

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, CK, TSH, B12, ANA, AChR antibodies, NMO-IgG
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All essentially normal; low-titer ANA and mild vitamin D insufficiency are incidental

optimal_actions.action: Consider lumbar puncture if MS remains in differential despite normal MRI
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CSF with no oligoclonal bands

optimal_actions.action: Recognize positive functional signs as diagnostic (not diagnosis of exclusion) and formulate FND diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diagnosis of functional neurological disorder based on positive clinical signs

optimal_actions.action: Communicate diagnosis to patient using transparent, non-dismissive approach emphasizing that symptoms are real and treatable
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understanding that FND is a real neurological condition

optimal_actions.action: Refer for specialized physiotherapy with FND expertise and psychiatric evaluation for comorbid depression, anxiety, and PTSD

critical_actions: Perform specific functional neurological examination signs (Hoover sign, hip abductor sign) to make a rule-in diagnosis rather than relying solely on exclusion
Exclude myelopathy and structural spinal cord disease with appropriate imaging
Exclude peripheral neuropathy and myopathy with EMG/NCS
Recognize the diagnosis of FND as based on positive clinical signs, not merely absence of findings
Communicate the diagnosis in a transparent, empathetic, non-dismissive manner
Do NOT attribute symptoms to malingering or fabrication
Refer for multidisciplinary treatment including specialized physiotherapy and psychiatric care
contraindicated_actions: Labeling the patient as a malingerer or stating symptoms are 'all in her head'
Prescribing antiepileptic medications without evidence of epilepsy
Ordering unnecessary invasive procedures (e.g., spinal surgery based on incidental lumbar findings)
Dismissing the patient without providing a clear diagnosis and treatment plan
Over-investigating with repeated imaging or unnecessary biopsies after comprehensive negative workup
Starting high-dose opioids for the associated chronic pain
Attributing all symptoms to psychiatric illness without acknowledging FND as a neurological diagnosis
key_reasoning_points: FND is diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, inconsistency), NOT by exclusion of other diseases. The 2013 DSM-5 criteria removed the requirement that symptoms be unexplained by neurological disease and instead require positive clinical findings of internal inconsistency.

The family history of MS and the low-titer ANA are red herrings. Low-titer ANA (1:80) is found in up to 15% of healthy individuals, and family history of MS does not make MS the diagnosis when brain and spinal cord MRI and CSF are entirely normal.
The mild vitamin D insufficiency is extremely common in the general population and does not explain bilateral leg weakness of this severity.
The lumbar disc herniations are stable, minimal, and not compressing nerve roots — they are incidental and should not be attributed as the cause of wheelchair-level bilateral weakness.

The EMG finding of reduced recruitment with normal MUP morphology and rapid firing rates is a hallmark of poor voluntary activation, not neurogenic disease. An experienced electromyographer would recognize this pattern.
The non-anatomical sensory examination (sharp demarcation at inguinal creases) does not correspond to any dermatomal level, peripheral nerve territory, or spinal cord level.

The psychiatric comorbidities (depression, anxiety, PTSD, childhood trauma) are important predisposing and perpetuating factors but do NOT mean the patient is malingering. FND involves involuntary symptom production.
The temporal association between escalating psychosocial stressors and symptom onset is a common but not required feature of FND. The diagnosis rests on the examination findings.

key_positive_signs: Inconsistency between observed and tested strength
